enonce.texte: Match each disease or health issue on the left with its common symptom(s) on the right. Write the letter of the correct answer.

questions.texte: Match the following diseases with their symptoms.

sousQuestions.reponse: d. Cough, fever, loss of taste or smell
b. Weakened immune system, weight loss, fatigue
c. Runny nose, sneezing, sore throat

intitule: Fill in the Blanks: Disease Prevention

enonce.texte:
Complete the sentences with the correct word from the box below.

Word box: vaccine, transmission, symptoms, immunity, outbreak, contagious, treatment, diagnosis

questions.texte: Fill in each blank with the appropriate term.

sousQuestions.texte: A(n) __________ is a substance that helps the body develop protection against a specific disease.
The __________ of COVID-19 includes fever, cough, and difficulty breathing.
Washing hands regularly reduces the __________ of infectious diseases.
After recovering from chickenpox, the body develops __________ against future infections.
An Ebola __________ occurred in West Africa between 2014 and 2016.

enonce.texte:
Read the short passage below and answer the questions that follow.

Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by parasites transmitted through the bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. Common symptoms include high fever, chills, headache, and vomiting. Prevention methods include sleeping under insecticide-treated nets and taking antimalarial drugs. On the other hand, HIV/AIDS attacks the immune system, making the body vulnerable to other infections. It is transmitted through contact with infected blood, unprotected sex, or from mother to child during childbirth. While there is no cure for HIV/AIDS, antiretroviral therapy can control the virus and prevent transmission. COVID-19, caused by the coronavirus, spreads through respiratory droplets. Symptoms range from mild to severe and include fever, cough, and loss of taste or smell. Vaccination has been a key tool in controlling the pandemic.

questions.texte: Compare and contrast the transmission methods of malaria and HIV/AIDS.
questions.reponse: Malaria is transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes, while HIV/AIDS is transmitted through contact with infected blood, unprotected sex, or mother-to-child during childbirth. Malaria is not transmitted from person to person directly, whereas HIV/AIDS requires direct fluid contact.

questions.texte: Explain why vaccination was effective against COVID-19 but there is no vaccine for HIV/AIDS mentioned in the passage.
questions.reponse: The passage mentions that there is no cure for HIV/AIDS, but antiretroviral therapy can control it. Developing a vaccine for HIV has been difficult because the virus mutates rapidly. COVID-19 vaccines were developed quickly because the virus is more stable and previous coronavirus research existed.

questions.texte: Evaluate the importance of early diagnosis for each of the three diseases mentioned.
questions.reponse: Early diagnosis of malaria allows for prompt treatment with antimalarial drugs, preventing severe complications and death. For HIV/AIDS, early diagnosis enables timely initiation of antiretroviral therapy, reducing viral load and preventing transmission. For COVID-19, early diagnosis helps isolate the patient, reduce spread, and provide appropriate care. Without early diagnosis, all three diseases can lead to severe health outcomes and increased community transmission.

enonce.texte: You are a community health worker in a rural village in Cameroon. A recent report shows an increase in malaria cases among children under five years old. Additionally, some adults show symptoms of HIV/AIDS but avoid getting tested because of stigma. Several families also report members with persistent cough and fever, possibly COVID-19.

questions.texte: Propose a three-step action plan to reduce malaria transmission in the village.
questions.reponse: Step 1: Distribute insecticide-treated mosquito nets to all households, especially those with children under five. Step 2: Organize a community clean-up campaign to eliminate standing water where mosquitoes breed. Step 3: Train local volunteers to recognize malaria symptoms and refer children to the nearest health center for rapid testing and treatment.

questions.texte: Design a community awareness campaign to encourage HIV/AIDS testing while reducing stigma. Include the target audience, key messages, and communication methods.
questions.reponse: Target audience: Adults aged 15-49. Key messages: 'Knowing your status saves lives,' 'HIV is not a death sentence – treatment works,' 'Testing is confidential and free.' Communication methods: Use local radio announcements, community meetings led by trusted elders, and peer educators. Also, involve people living with HIV who can share positive stories about treatment success.

questions.texte: Create a decision-making flowchart for a family member who has a persistent cough and fever. Include when to stay home, when to visit a clinic, and when to seek emergency care.
questions.reponse: Flowchart: Start → Symptom: cough + fever → If mild and no breathing difficulty → Stay home, wear mask, isolate, monitor symptoms → If symptoms worsen after 2 days OR high fever persists → Visit clinic for COVID-19/malaria testing → If difficulty breathing, chest pain, or confusion → Seek emergency care immediately → After testing: If positive for COVID-19 → Isolate for 7-10 days, treat symptoms → If positive for malaria → Take antimalarial drugs → If symptoms resolve → Return to normal activities with caution.

intitule: Health Vocabulary in Context: Dialogue Completion

enonce.texte:
Complete the dialogue between a doctor (Dr. Njoya) and a patient (Fatima) using the words from the box.

Word box: symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, contagious, fever, vaccine, immunity, malaria, HIV, cough

questions.texte: Fill in each blank with the correct word to complete the conversation.

sousQuestions.texte: Dr. Njoya: "Good morning, Fatima. What __________ are you experiencing?"
Fatima: "I have a high __________, a persistent __________, and I feel very tired."
Dr. Njoya: "Have you traveled recently? This could be __________ or COVID-19. We need to run a test for a proper __________."
Fatima: "Is this disease __________? I don't want to infect my children."
Dr. Njoya: "COVID-19 is highly contagious. Have you received the __________? It helps build __________ against severe illness. For now, I will prescribe a __________ plan."

enonce.texte: Imagine your school has recorded three cases of COVID-19 and two cases of malaria among students in the past week. The principal has asked you, as the head of the Health Club, to write a public health announcement to be read during morning assembly.

questions.texte: Write a public health announcement (150-200 words) that includes: (a) a description of the situation, (b) preventive measures for both COVID-19 and malaria, (c) what students should do if they have symptoms, and (d) a call to action.
questions.reponse: Sample answer: 'Good morning students. The Health Club has confirmed three COVID-19 cases and two malaria cases in our school this week. We need everyone's cooperation to prevent further spread. For COVID-19, wear your masks properly, wash hands regularly, and maintain distance. For malaria, sleep under treated mosquito nets and eliminate stagnant water around dormitories. If you have fever, cough, headache, or body aches, report immediately to the school nurse. Do not self-medicate or hide your symptoms. Let's protect ourselves and our friends. Stay safe, stay healthy!'

enonce.texte: Create a research card for each of the following diseases by completing the table in your answer sheet. You must identify: cause, transmission method, common symptoms, prevention, and treatment.

questions.texte: Complete the research card for Malaria.
questions.reponse: Cause: Plasmodium parasite. Transmission: Bite of infected female Anopheles mosquito. Symptoms: Fever, chills, headache, vomiting, sweating. Prevention: Insecticide-treated nets, indoor spraying, antimalarial drugs. Treatment: Antimalarial medication (e.g., artemisinin-based combination therapy).

questions.texte: Complete the research card for HIV/AIDS.
questions.reponse: Cause: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). Transmission: Unprotected sex, infected blood, mother to child during birth/breastfeeding. Symptoms: Weak immune system, weight loss, fatigue, recurrent infections. Prevention: Condom use, sterile needles, antiretroviral drugs for pregnant mothers. Treatment: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) – no cure but controls the virus.

questions.texte: Complete the research card for COVID-19.
questions.reponse: Cause: SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. Transmission: Respiratory droplets, airborne, contact with contaminated surfaces. Symptoms: Fever, cough, loss of taste/smell, difficulty breathing. Prevention: Vaccination, mask-wearing, hand hygiene, physical distancing. Treatment: Supportive care, antivirals, oxygen therapy for severe cases.
